lesson: L14 — Volume of Rectangular Solids & Cylinders
essential_question: If area covers a flat shape with one number, what carries that same idea into three dimensions to fill a solid?
objective: State that volume = area of the base × height, apply it to find the volume of a rectangular solid (V = l·w·h) and a cylinder (V = πr²·h), identify a solid's faces, edges, and vertices, and express volume in cubic units.

content.prompt: Picture a cereal box or a soup can sitting in front of you. Area told you how much surface a flat shape covers — but a box like this has empty space *inside* it. Without measuring anything, what do you think you'd need to know to say exactly how many little cubes it would take to fill it completely?

content.heading: One rule builds every volume formula in this lesson
bullets: Volume is the amount of space a solid fills, measured in CUBIC units (in³, ft³, cm³) — never square or linear units.
The master rule: Volume = area of the base × height — Math-U-See's own words, and it works for any solid with a flat base and straight, perpendicular sides.
A rectangular solid's base is a rectangle (area = l·w), so V = l·w·h. A cube is a rectangular solid where l = w = h, so V = s³.
A cylinder's base is a circle (area = πr²), so V = πr²·h — the curved side runs perpendicular to the base, same as a box's upright sides.
A rectangular solid has 6 flat faces, 12 edges (where two faces meet), and 8 vertices (where edges meet).

content.heading: Change the solid; watch the volume fill in
content.note: On the sim, choose Box or Cylinder. Drag the length, width, and height sliders (or the radius and height sliders for the cylinder) and watch the readout update live with the base area, the height, and the formula plugged in with real numbers. Predict the volume before you drag, then check.

content.preview: Build session: measure and compute the volume of real boxes and cylinders — or 3D-print/CAD-model a solid and verify it by water displacement or unit cubes.

success_criteria.text: I can state that volume = area of the base × height and identify a solid's faces, edges, and vertices
I can find the volume of a rectangular solid using V = l·w·h
I can find the volume of a cylinder using V = πr²·h, and explain why volume is measured in cubic units

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W16 Volume of Solids.md

questions.prompt: The master formula for the volume of a solid is area of the ___ times the height.
questions.explain: Math-U-See's own rule: volume = area of the base × the height — not the perimeter, a diagonal, or the circumference.

questions.prompt: The lines where the faces of a rectangular solid meet are called its…
questions.explain: Edges are the segments where two faces meet; the points where edges meet are vertices.

questions.prompt: A rectangular solid has length 8 cm, width 5 cm, and height 6 cm. Find its volume.
A cube has edges 3 in long. Find its volume.
questions.explain: A cube's volume is edge³: 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 in³ (the same as V = l·w·h with l = w = h = 3).

questions.prompt: A cylinder has a radius of 4 ft and a height of 10 ft. Find its volume in terms of π, then estimate it as a decimal (use π ≈ 3.14).
On the grid, sketch a rectangular solid (an oblique box) and label its length, width, and height. Then mark and label one face, one edge, and one vertex on your drawing.
questions.answer: A labeled 3-D box with l, w, h marked, plus one face, one edge, and one vertex each clearly identified.
questions.explain: Rubric: a recognizable 3-D box with all three dimensions labeled (1 pt) + a face, an edge, and a vertex each correctly identified (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Exit: name one container from your day (a cereal box, a soup can, a moving box, a water bottle) and say whether it's closer to a rectangular solid or a cylinder — and which measurements (length/width/height, or radius/height) you'd need to find its volume.
